Transcrição Completa: Low Ticket To High Ticket Mastery
[00:00] All right. So, low ticket to high ticket mastery. First of all, awesome topic. Gonna be going through some examples, um, some best practices, just making sure you guys walk away with as much value as possible and being able to execute it with simplicity.
[00:15] Um, first things first, let me make it really clear what we're talking about, okay? We're talking about taking somebody from ideally tens of dollars. Sometimes you could even sell it for like a hundred to $200 for the first product.
[00:28] And you have a few different upsells throughout the funnel itself. You typically end these funnels with a book a call or in some instances you can even end them with an opt-in to a webinar as an example.
[00:40] But the whole intention of the low ticket funnel is to acquire a buyer lead. Buyer leads are historically higher value and have higher odds give or take what they buy into upselling to something that's higher ticket, which in most instances is going to be the main thing that you actually want to sell.
[00:58] It also obviously gives us an opportunity to liquidate our ad dollars. So, in comparison to call funnels or webinar funnels or even like free challenge funnels, DM strategies, obviously in those scenarios, we're fronting cash for our average sales cycle timeline at which point we make the money back and are officially profitable.
[01:18] The most ideal scenario with low ticket to high ticket need I not say it out loud is to become profitable going into the upsell process of getting that person the high ticket product.
[01:30] We in most instances are going to shoot for profitability. However, we might break even. We might even lose a few dollars. Like we have a client right now, they're doing just shy of 1.6 mil a month.
[01:42] They do a little more than 1.1 mil a month off of a bookfunnel, low ticket to high ticket ascension. I'm unfortunately under NDA with that deal, so I can't show you all their funnels and stuff.
[01:54] However, they have three different upsells and their AOV is usually a couple dollars less than what we actually get as a cost per purchase. So, they lose a few dollars for every book buyer lead that they get.
[02:08] However, the average sales cycle timeline for a $10,000 to $35,000 product with a $20,000 option in between as well is only nine days on average.
[02:20] So, would you front a few dollars for nine days at a time in order to make back damn near pure profit minus commissions and obviously cost of goods sold? I mean, I would, they do, and a lot of other people do, too.
[02:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "What do you currently have demand for that you aren't delivering in a cost effective way?"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Pergunta central em destaque, prompt mental para identificação de produtos low ticket
[02:45] So first thing that you have to be thinking with is like what are you actually going to sell? You know, like what is the low ticket thing? A lot of people like to think with like random shit okay? And you don't want to think with random shit.
[02:58] The way that you want to pull what the front end low ticket thing should be is by thinking with this question. Okay, this is like the mental prompt you want to use. What do you currently have demand for that you aren't delivering in a cost effective way?
[03:15] So to be clear, when you think with this specific question, usually you can look at your customer base. You can look at the sales calls that you get. You can reflect on things that people are asking you questions about currently.
[03:28] And you could very easily come up with this kind of thing. Simply put, it shouldn't feel like a guess. You know, in no way, shape, or form should it feel like a guess.
[03:38] It should feel more like you're just filling a set of demands that is clearly there that again you just aren't cost-effectively selling into right now and it's all just based on reflecting what I know that people want from me.
[03:52] So let me give you an example of this. Okay. Um you know for my audience we consistently get people that want our content ad strategies. Okay. These could be things like the Venus fly trap, the harvester, the tornado, uh the hammer them strategy, and you know the dozens of others.
[04:10] Point is that is an example is is literally one of the funnels that I'm going to show you. It's a breakout strategy. It's something that we can easily take from the main thing, deliver to people in a cost effective way, okay, and teach people, add a few upsells to it.
[04:28] And that clearly plays into the main things that I could sell somebody like master internet marketing or like the inner circle as an example, or in some instances, they might even become an agency client.
[04:40] I could do the same thing with like CallFunnel Mastery. Call funnels when we look at my YouTube analytics as an example are one of the types of video topics that generally get the most momentum the fastest and are some of my most viewed videos.
[04:55] So callfunnel mastery as an example would be a great like mini-course that I could easily create a low ticket product for. I could do the same thing with webinars. I could do the same thing with challenge funnels. I could even do the same thing with this right here of low ticket to high ticket.
[05:12] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Pricing Strategy Matters"
NÚMEROS: $60K/year (Inner Circle), $5K one-time (Master Internet Marketing)
VISUAL: Comparação de preços, estrutura de ofertas, hierarquia de produtos
[05:20] Now, let me be clear when I say this. The pricing strategy matters a lot. So, when we think of how we're actually going to charge people, okay, that all has to do with the upsells.
[05:32] If I have something that all the way down at the end of this whole ascension model is let's say like mine as an example, it's 60k a year to be a part of the inner circle.
[05:42] Easy money to make back, you know, I'm going to teach how many one things that can make back that entire amount in a month, you know, let alone all the one things that people learn over the course of an entire year.
[05:54] However, again, on the front end, it's like I can still sell stuff that teach people things that help them get results just like I do on YouTube. It makes it easier to trust me.
[06:05] The other thing that we have as another option is the $5,000 one-time price, and that's for the master internet marketing offer. Okay. So relative to the prices that are at the end of the funnel like the where the actual ascension occurs that kind of dictates very strongly what I need to charge on the front end.
[06:25] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Price = Signal of Quality"
NÚMEROS: $10 (book), $5K, $60K
VISUAL: Escala de preços, conceito de sinalização de qualidade, atração de público qualificado
[06:30] Okay. I don't want to attract brokies and as we know price is one of the ultimate signals of quality and expected result. Okay. So if I go out there and I sell like as an example I have a book.
[06:45] Most people don't even know this because I never really promote it. It's called What I Wish I Knew Getting Started and it's a great read. It's maybe like a one to two hour read and it's great, very action-oriented, very result-oriented.
[07:00] I get consistently awesome feedback on it. I give it away for free in a lead magnet. I also have it on Amazon. I don't think there's any physical copies anymore. I haven't reordered them for a while. However, it's on Kindle and it's got an Audible version, too.
[07:15] It's like that's like 10 bucks, you know? Again, is that the best thing for me to lead with when I want to eventually sell somebody a 60K a year offer or a 5K one-time offer? Well, it depends on the framing.
[07:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Frame Matters"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados diretamente
VISUAL: Conceito de framing, contexto de compra, sinalização de comportamento do comprador
[07:35] Okay? So, this is the other key variable we got to think with when we're looking at what to actually sell. Okay? So, as an example, back in the day when I worked at Grant Cardone's office, we had dozens and dozens and dozens of these like low ticket products.
[07:52] I'll give you a really good one as an example. We had a jar of wristbands. We also had single wristbands. And by wristbands, does everybody remember those little uh they're like the little rubber bands?
[08:05] It's it's actually a matter of fact, if you come to one of our masterminds here for the inner circle that says that little band that we give you that says mastermind access and Jeremy's inner circle on it with the logos, it's like those.
[08:18] But Grant would have these things with messaging on it. Like he had one that said like don't be a little bitch. Uh no negativity, like just like certain, you know, uh you can call them like culture messages, okay?
[08:32] And he would sell these individually, but more importantly, he would sell a jar of them. And by a jar, I mean it'd have like a hundred in there. Okay, here. What do you think about this?
[08:45] If somebody bought a jar of wristbands, what is that signal? Right? Somebody unmute yourself or somebody dropping in the chat. If you bought a jar of wristbands, what does that likely signal about the buyer?
[09:00] Jonas answers it. They have a team. That's exactly right.
[09:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Signal Analysis: Jar vs Single Wristband"
NÚMEROS: $1,000 per additional team member, $5K base price
VISUAL: Comparação visual entre compra individual vs bulk, sinalização de tamanho de equipe
[09:10] So, when you look at the probability of somebody buying something for $5,000 or somebody buying something for $60,000, it's like, well, there's a really good probability that we're going to be able to upsell a person who buys a jar of wristbands because it's a signal.
[09:28] Like the frame of what they're buying is something that matters. It shows the salesperson, shows the business what the person's probable to buy.
[09:38] So, it's like, think about this. I have at that 5K one-time price, we add $1,000 for every additional team member that they want to add to the product.
[09:50] So, if Leo, as an example, let's say that we sold wristbands and somebody bought a single wristband. It's like the single wristband would make more sense to call and talk to that person about the 5K master internet marketing course.
[10:05] Versus if somebody bought a jar of wristbands. His conversation needs to immediately be, "Well, how many team members do you want to have go through this training as well? I assume it's not just one person. I assume you're going to want to put a handful of people through here. We'll do a discount for you every additional person you add."
[10:25] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Grant Cardone's Low Ticket System"
NÚMEROS: Vários inches de pedidos diários impressos
VISUAL: Stack de pedidos físicos, processo de distribuição para closers, sistema de vendas em escala
[10:30] Okay. We made a lot of money back in the day at Grant's office selling these lower ticket things. Every morning there would be a giant stack of orders from the previous day that would get printed like several inches tall.
[10:45] Print them off, take them into the sales team. Head of sales would sit there, flick through them, and distribute them out to all the closers that were there in the business.
[10:55] Okay? And then from there, that would be some of the first calls that they would make of the day. They also had things like these MP3s of the book. Okay? And there were several books that he had.
[11:08] Some of the books, as we know, are products like the 10X rule, but he had books on sales, too. Lot of books on sales. Same kind of logic. The frame of what they bought would dictate what the closer would call them up and try to upsell them on.
[11:25] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Price & Frame = Upsell Potential"
NÚMEROS: $50-$70 vs $100-$197, $99 (jar), $50 (MP3 bundle)
VISUAL: Matriz de preço e frame, correlação com probabilidade de upsell
[11:35] Okay. So again, when you look at these two different variables, price and frame, they matter a lot to what you're actually probable to put on the back end that they should buy them.
[11:48] They also matter a lot to the sales team. Okay? So understand what I'm saying here. You have to have a sales team that's fully aware of how what the person buys dictates what they should be upsold and talked about when that setter finally dials.

[12:25] And then you could get a physical version too as as as well as the digital version for $197. Those were the front-end leading price points for those two specific offers.

[12:50] Again, we'll look at those here in a moment. But my point is is like if I tried to sell something for 10 bucks right away, I'm not going to have a good time, you know?
[13:00] I'm actually going to have a bad time because I'm going to attract people who have a lower probability to buy my higher ticket things that I want them to later purchase.

[13:45] They had closed Sprint off of a bundle of MP3s, a low ticket to high ticket upsell Ascension for millions of dollars in annual contract value.
[14:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Sweet Spot: $70-$150"
NÚMEROS: $20-$30 (baixa conversão), $70-$150 (sweet spot)
VISUAL: Range de preços ideal destacado, psicologia de preço, thresholds de ativação
[14:10] Okay, so keep that in mind for the price. Um, Lewis asked, "Is that price the same for B2B and B TO C?" Now listen, I fucking hate terms like that. That's bullshit. Don't ask me words like that, okay?
[14:25] Start talking in rich and poor. Okay, rich and poor is what you actually want to be talking about. It doesn't matter at all if it's a B2B or a B TO C product for rich people or a B2B or a B TO C product for poor people.
[14:40] Okay, the way that you sell is based on whether they are rich or whether they're in the general public. Okay? So again, if you're selling to the general public, then yes, some of these prices again, but the lessons that I'm talking about are the same across the board.
[14:58] Even if you're selling to the general public, these prices of what I'm talking about, dictate upsell potential. Okay? If you want a higher probability to be profitable on the front end, you want a higher probability to actually upsell a majority of these folks, you still want to be in these price ranges that I'm talking about.
[15:18] The good news is rich people take these things serious, too. Okay, back to the point at hand.
[15:25] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Upsell Page Best Practices"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Layout de página de upsell, acknowledgment de compra, estrutura de comunicação
[15:30] So, after somebody buys, they get taken to this page. So, this page, this is an upsell here where the very first thing you want to do, a lot of people get this wrong, okay?
[15:42] They do this super whack shit where they put like a giant stop sign at the top of the page and THEY'RE LIKE, "STOP. LIKE, WATCH THIS VIDEO BEFORE YOU CONTINUE." LIKE, don't fucking do that, okay? Don't fucking do that.
[15:58] That is like super aggressive and makes no sense to do, okay? You're like immediately creating anxiousness right away. And there's a it's like those motherfuckers that run ads THAT ARE LIKE, "STOP SCROLLING. PAY ATTENTION." IT'S LIKE, "NO, NO. We're going to keep scrolling, dog."
[16:18] Okay? It's going to be the same thing here. You want to start by congratulating them on the purchase. Okay? Actually acknowledge they bought the thing. Tell them they are going to get the thing in their email in a few minutes.
[16:32] Okay? If you don't, you don't understand how the human brain works. The human brain is not going to be attentive to what you're trying to sell them here if you do not actually acknowledge that the thing that they just did happened.
[16:48] The brain will be tweaking out wondering if it happened or not. Okay? Make sure you acknowledge it. Say it at the beginning of the video, too.

[18:35] Okay, that's congruency. Look at that little smile right there. Fucking yeah, dude. You're right to be smiling. 17% take rate on $147 first upsell.
[18:48] You know, most people like that bookfunnel is an example I was telling you guys about for the client that does about 1.1 mil off their BookFunnel right now out of 1.6. Dude, their take rate on their first upsell is like 3%.
[19:05] It's like, well, why? Because it just doesn't make sense. Like, most people over complicate the shit out of the upsells. It's like, if I sell a book, the first upsell should be like the audio version of the book, you know.
[19:20] Or the first upsell should be like just like this, like, "Hey, man, I have a whole video explainer for each chapter in the book. [24:02] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "WRONG: Going Backwards"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Seta voltando do branch para o centro, X vermelho, demonstração de erro estratégico
[24:10] So, it's like all I'm doing is I'm not selling. And this is where a lot of people get this shit wrong. Like this is what my clients doing wrong as an example.
[24:20] They chose the path of the content ad strategies, but instead of following the breakout path that makes the most sense after they went from here to like upselling callfunnel mastery and then from there to upselling challenge mastery.
[24:35] It just doesn't make any there's no congruency. You see, there's no congruency to that. If you want a really high take rate on your upsells, you have to branch off from the thing that you sold them initially.
[24:50] You don't go backwards. Notice the direction of this. It's like, okay, I sell them this, then I go out. You don't go here and then come back to the main thing. No, you keep going out and you branch into what makes sense that relates to the thing you branched out from.
[25:10] You don't go backwards to the main thing and look for other stuff to upsell. Does that make sense? You find complimenting branch products to upsell. Very critical lesson.
[25:25] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Second Upsell: Mastermind Bundle"

[25:50] One of the places where I've talked at the highest levels is at masterminds. I did these talks at a Tai Lopez mastermind. People paid 10K to go. I paid I also did this talk at Garrett Jay White's mastermind. People paid 20K to go.
[26:08] I talk at the highest fucking most advanced level at masterminds, okay? I understand what they're for and I try to really show out at masterminds. Okay?

[26:38] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Declining Take Rates"
NÚMEROS: Step 1: 17%, Step 2: 7%
VISUAL: Gráfico descendente, cada passo com menor conversão, conceito de budget depletion
[26:45] Okay? I want to be really clear when I say this. Each step that you continue onward in the low ticket to high ticket flow has a lower take rate than the previous step.
[27:00] Okay. Somebody on the front end was thinking to themselves, "Oh, 100 bucks, 197 bucks, dude, that's sick. Like, I'll buy that." Then they get here and they're like, "Oh, another 147 bucks." Like, yeah. you know, like that makes sense.
[27:18] Like it does make sense to have the videos. They get here and then they're like, "Ah, fuck dude. That sounds good, but like, you know, I'm kind of tapped out."
[27:28] So, like, you lose a lot of people each step you go onward. And it's a very simple reason why. They just have less money to spend with you as you get into the steps that follow.
[27:42] Because, keep in mind, the logic of what I said makes a lot of sense. They have to and they want to get a result with what they've originally purchased before they're generally willing to give you a lot more money.
[27:58] Very important to consider what we place as our upsells. We want to make sure that we strategically order these things.
[28:08] So, to be clear, upsell one, the video explainers, that's the easiest layup deal of an upsell ever. That's a lot easier, congruent, makes more sense to buy with this compared to the mastermind bundle.
[28:25] The mastermind bundle makes sense, too, but it just doesn't make as much sense as the explainer videos of the strategies. You see, we're getting a little further away with the mastermind bundle, even though I talk about the content ad strategies at the masterminds, okay?
[28:42] And even though there's a tremendous amount of value there because you're learning something that other people paid tens of thousands of dollars for for 197 bucks, you're just getting further away from the thing that they actually originally purchased.
[28:58] And you're also extracting more money from them already. Okay? So again, people just don't have that much for you each step that you continue on in the funnel.
[29:12] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Upsell Page Styling"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Layout limpo, headline, vídeo, copy, dois botões de compra, botão de recusa
[29:20] Okay? Now again, just in terms of style of the page here, you'll notice I already acknowledged on the first upsell that they bought. Okay? And I already said that at the beginning of this video.
[29:32] I don't need to say that here. Like you'll notice in this video I'm not sitting there like, hey, you know, congratulate. I don't need to say any of that shit. I don't need to say it on the page. They know what's good now. Okay?
[29:48] Now it's fucking upsell time. It's give me more money time. Okay. Same exact flow. headline, video, copy, two buy buttons. No, I don't want to buy. Okay, super simple. Easy.
[30:05] These should not be like super long sales pages. I do want to encourage as well if you happen to have maybe like a handful of testimonials about whatever that specific upsell is, that's cool.

[55:48] Now they're never going to fucking answer your call again unless they actually want that in a few days time which to be fair will happen but it won't happen as often as if you take the first approach.

[58:25] Okay, how long does it take on average for me to get somebody to buy this content first ad strategies book and get them to the point where they actually apply it, use it, and are officially ready to give us more money from how fucking awesome it was.
[58:45] I need to know that time frame. If I don't know that time frame, I'm going to impede. I'm going to encroach. I'm gonna be premature. If I wait too long, I'm gonna miss the perfect window.

[01:02:20] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Don't Jump Off The Bridge"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Metáfora visual de bridge, perigo de upsell prematuro
[01:02:25] Okay? If I try to ascend somebody while they're on the bridge, they're jumping off the bridge. Nobody jumps off bridges. Okay? They want to get to the other side of the bridge.
[01:02:40] So, this amount of time, this can vary. It's like, guys, sometimes in the sometimes in master internet marketing, like that guy, it could take 30 days. The 5K thing in that example acts as the low ticket to high ticket ascension with a with a breakout product.
[01:03:00] Here's the cool thing, right? With a breakout product, something like this, it's like the result bridge is going to be a lot shorter. It's going to be a lot lot shorter in terms of the duration of time. Might be seven days. You got to know that time frame. You got to know it. You cannot omit that. You have to know.
[01:03:22] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Value Communication Increases Show-Up Rate"
NÚMEROS: $1,200 (baseball tickets), 3 semanas antecedência
VISUAL: Exemplo pessoal de não-comparecimento por falta de comunicação
[01:03:30] Here's the thing. These, okay, the value dense emails, the hammer them, the setters, that increases the odds that you're going to show up to Disney. Okay?
[01:03:45] You know, just the other day, I'd spent $1,200 to go to a baseball game. It was on Tuesday. I had I had spent that money though like three weeks ago. I wanted to go see Shoai Otani.
[01:04:00] Shoai Otani is a baseball player on the Los Angeles Dodgers. He got a $700 million 10-year contract with the Los Angeles Dodgers. They just won the World Series last year. There are some other great players on that team.
[01:04:18] But hey, man, you give a guy $700 million in sports, I'll fucking pay to go watch him no matter what sport he plays. I don't care. I'll go watch that shit. I even like watching baseball.
[01:04:32] I bought the seats literally right behind home plate. Like the literal first seats smack in the middle right behind the umpire at home plate. And I forgot to go.
[01:04:48] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Zero Communication = Zero Show-Up"
NÚMEROS: $700M (contrato Ohtani), $1,200 (ingressos perdidos)
VISUAL: Falta total de pre-game communication, resultado em não-comparecimento
[01:04:52] You know why I forgot to go? I didn't get any content about showdy coming into town, man. I didn't see anything on social media where I spend all my time. I didn't get a single fucking email, hyping me up, telling me the game's coming up.
[01:05:10] Nobody from the Miami fucking whatever they're called called me and say, "Hey, man. Excited to see you at the game coming up." I didn't even get a fucking text. I got nothing.
[01:05:25] So, come Tuesday, I'm in the middle of doing a bunch of work shit. And then my ticket guy that I bought from calls me around like two hours after the game started and he's like, "Bro, did you forget to go to the game?"
[01:05:42] And I was like, "Fuck." Yeah, I did. Did the Miami Marlins make more money from me that night? Nope. They got money from me, but they didn't help me cross the bridge, so they didn't get me more money. They didn't get anything else from me.
[01:06:00] Would I have spent money if I would have gone to the game? Yeah, dude. The last game I went to, my fiance was bitching about being cold. I had to buy her a sweater.
[01:06:15] I naturally bought like I didn't I'm not a fucking hot dog guy or anything to be clear. I think I bought like some kind of pulled pork thing. I had also got a uh can't remember what it was. It was this like giant fucking popcorn thing. It was pretty cool.
[01:06:32] Anyway, my point is like I spent a few more dollars in that scenario. Buying the ticket going to the game is the result and then from there they could have upsold the shit out of me.
[01:06:48] The messaging there would have been the people walking AROUND THE STANDS HOT DOGS, YOU KNOW, fucking people like that. You see what I mean? I could have bought more stuff for sure, but they couldn't get me to the point of the result. You see?

NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Analogia completa: ticket ≠ park attendance ≠ upsells, bridge necessário
[01:07:12] Would it make any sense for me to buy anything from the fucking baseball game if I'm just up here in the pee house? It only makes sense for me to buy things from the baseball game if I go to the fucking baseball game. Okay?
[01:07:28] I'm not buying all this shit at Disneyland or Disney World unless I go to fucking Disneyland or Disney World. You see, I'm not going to buy your upsell if you don't get me a result with whatever the fuck the thing was that I bought in the first place.

[01:08:08] We go from them buying something. Okay, even if they only buy one thing, doesn't matter. If they buy the upsells, that's cool, too. Don't care. All the initial communication is get a fucking result with the thing you just bought.

[01:08:42] That's when I'm officially trying to ascend you, get more money out of you, and help you out more. See what I mean? Easy fucking game.

[01:09:32] Or whatever fucking platform invoicing plan you're using, waiting to get those dollars back to pay that off and then put some money in your pocket.
[01:09:45] Ideally, you're profitable here. That way, you can do this part a little more confidently instead of desperately. And then we transition everything over.

NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Layout de receipt + acknowledgment video + VSSL/webinar optin section
[01:11:55] Now, here's the thing. Let me be really clear on the price that I've selected here. You know how a lot of people will try to get profitable from the upsells like by increasing the AOV instead of just trying to be profitable on the front end.
[01:12:12] I just ran a test where I was like, you know what? If I could just try to be profitable on the first purchase and then I just get even more profitable on the backend purchases.
[01:12:28] You know, what would I need to price my front end? blowing my conversion statistics need to be on the front end in order to pull that off. That test worked out great.
[01:12:45] We would generally be just just above break even or in some instances very profitable give or take the uh day of the week, holidays, time of year, all that fun stuff. You guys know how ad costs fluctuate, fatigue cycles, etc.
[01:13:05] Then the other two upsells that followed this, it's pretty much pure profit. generally worked really well.
[01:13:18] The other thing that I was considering with this specific price, like I told you guys in the example I I provided when we were looking through the notes there for me, I wanted price to be one of those initial signals that like the things I have to upsell aren't going to be cheap.
[01:13:38] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Psychology Threshold: $20-$30"
NÚMEROS: $20-$30 = baixíssima take rate em upsells
VISUAL: Range problemático destacado, "fruits & vegetables pricing"
[01:13:45] I generally find and this is a this is like a psychology threshold here is what we're going to call it. Anything where people are buying something for like 20 bucks, we have a wide discrepancy in statistics here.
[01:14:02] Anything that's around like 20 to $30, there's generally a very low quantity of people that buy the upsells, no matter what the upsells are. Make sure you take note of what I just said.
[01:14:18] Anything that's around 20 to $30, generally you get a very low take rate on any of the upsells, no matter how good the upsells are.
[01:14:32] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Fruits & Vegetables Pricing"
NÚMEROS: $20-$30
VISUAL: Metáfora de compras de supermercado, ciclo de desperdício, falta de compromisso
[01:14:38] $ 20 to $30 is also fruits and vegetables pricing. Okay, we've talked about this many times. We'll talk about it again now.
[01:14:50] Fruits and vegetables pricing means, okay, you've gone and you brought fruits and vegetables in your lifetime where it sits in your fridge, they eventually go bad, you throw them away.
[01:15:05] Next time you order groceries, bang, you order same fruits and vegetables again and you repeat the cycle. Sometimes you eat them, but you don't feel obligated to eat them because you didn't spend enough.
[01:15:22] The one thing that happens with the fruits and vegetables analogy is that generally the thing you sell for low ticket prevents the high ticket sale from occurring until they get a result with the low ticket thing they purchased.
[01:15:40] So, as an example, if I sell a book, most people will say, "I want to read the book first and see if I get results with it."
[01:15:52] Or enough time has to pass where they're not taking the book seriously and they're not reading the book to then consider the upsell.
[01:16:05] They either want to see a result with what they've bought or they want enough time of exposing themselves to the fact they're not going to do anything with it, the fruits and vegetables analogy, to then say, "I'm considerate of the upsell now."
[01:16:25] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Quick Activation Cycle Required"
NÚMEROS: $70-$150 = sweet spot para ativação rápida
VISUAL: Correlation entre preço e seriedade de uso
[01:16:32] So, whatever you're going to whatever you're going to sell has to have a really quick activation cycle. And I like to think that price is something that's going to dictate whether people use it in a tight time frame or not. This matters a lot.
[01:16:50] Okay, really think about this. If I sell it too cheap just to get more buyers, I'm not actually really helping myself because I'm just going to have a really long cycle if it takes a long time to activate those customers. See what I mean?
[01:17:08] If I sell it for enough, and enough could be like, we've seen this very commonly to be clear, somewhere around like the $70 to $150 range, that seems to be the sweet spot for the front-end low ticket offer.
[01:17:25] Somewhere between like $70 and $150. It makes them take it serious enough for a majority of people, meaning more than half, to log into it, use it, apply it, and attempt to get a result with it.
[01:17:45] You get a lot more serious people in that range. You also have a much higher take rate on your upsells when the first thing is between that $70 and $150 price.
[01:18:00] Think about this, right? The people only have like 20 to 30 bucks. You really think they're going to have enough for like a couple hundred in upsells on the back end? A lot of the times, no.
[01:18:18] It's also important to note the lower ticket things like in that 20 to $30 range in that example, they don't attract serious buyers either.
[01:18:32] Serious buyers don't think that a 20 to $30 thing is going to get them a good result. But for some reason, there's a big difference between 70 and $150 comparatively.
[01:18:48] Even a serious person will think something between 70 and $150 bucks can get them somewhere. See what I mean? It's like just a mental threshold there. It's like a psychology threshold is what we call that.
[01:19:05] So anyway, somewhere between 70 and 150, you get a good activation, get a lot of people that take it serious, you typically attract a higher quality individual who has a higher probability to buy the upsells and to ascend.
[01:19:25] Okay, so keep that in mind for the price. Um, Lewis asked, "Is that price the same for B2B and B TOC?" Now listen, I fucking hate terms like that. That's bullshit. Don't ask me words like that, okay?
[01:19:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Rich vs Poor (Not B2B vs B2C)"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Reframe de segmentação, riqueza como fator determinante
[01:19:50] Start talking in rich and poor. Okay, rich and poor is what you actually want to be talking about. It doesn't matter at all if it's a B2B or a B TO C product for rich people or a B2B or a B TO C product for poor people.
[01:20:10] Okay, the way that you sell is based on whether they are rich or whether they're in the general public. Okay?
[01:20:22] So again, if you're selling to the general public, then yes, some of these prices again, but the lessons that I'm talking about are the same across the board.
[01:20:35] Even if you're selling to the general public, these prices of what I'm talking about, dictate upsell potential. Okay?
[01:20:48] If you want a higher probability to be profitable on the front end, you want a higher probability to actually upsell a majority of these folks, you still want to be in these price ranges that I'm talking about.
[01:21:05] The good news is rich people take these things serious, too. Okay, back to the point at hand.

📊 RESUMO EXECUTIVO
NÚMEROS PRINCIPAIS:
$1.6M/mês (cliente com book funnel)
$1.1M/mês (apenas do low ticket to high ticket)
$10K-$35K (produtos high ticket típicos)
$20K (opção intermediária)
9 dias (ciclo médio de vendas)
17% (take rate no primeiro upsell - explainer videos)
7% (take rate no segundo upsell - mastermind bundle)
3% (take rate típica de book funnels mal estruturados)
$100 (preço digital do produto exemplo)
$197 (preço físico + digital)
$147 (primeiro upsell - vídeos explicativos)
$197 (segundo upsell - mastermind bundle)
$70-$150 (sweet spot de preço para low ticket)
$20-$30 (range problemático - "fruits & vegetables pricing")
$5K (Master Internet Marketing)
$60K/ano (Inner Circle)
$104K (resultado de cliente em 30 dias)
$1,200 (exemplo de ticket de baseball perdido)
$500 (Disney food pass exemplo)
10 dias (timing para pedir testimonial)
5 vídeos necessários (menos de 1 hora de gravação total)
ESTRATÉGIAS E SISTEMAS:
Low Ticket to High Ticket Framework:
Produto inicial $70-$150 (sweet spot)
Múltiplos upsells congruentes
Finalizar com book a call ou webinar optin
Objetivo: liquidar ad spend e criar buyer leads
Estrutura de Upsells (Branch Strategy):
Primeiro upsell: complemento direto (vídeos explicativos)
Segundo upsell: expandir no mesmo tema (mastermind bundle)
NUNCA voltar ao "main thing" - sempre branch para fora
Cada step tem take rate menor que o anterior
Pricing Psychology:
Preço = sinal de qualidade
Frame = sinal de comportamento (jar vs single wristband)
$20-$30 = zona morta (baixíssima conversão em upsells)
Rich vs Poor (não B2B vs B2C)
Post-Purchase Ascension (3-Pronged):
Value-dense email sequence
Hammer Them campaigns
Setters dialing (Educator Sales Role)
The Result Bridge:
Período entre compra e resultado
Comunicação 100% value durante bridge
Transição para sales APENAS após resultado
Activation window varia por produto (7-30+ dias)
Disney Analogy System:
Ticket = low ticket product
Getting to park = activation/result
Upsells at park = high ticket ascension
Communication pre-arrival = value emails
Educator Sales Role:
Person Type 1: consome todo conteúdo sozinho
Person Type 2: precisa de sales education
Primeira call: helper stance, não sales stance
"Just making sure you use what you bought"
Content Delivery Multiple Ways:
Nem todos leem o livro
Extrair conteúdo para emails
Hammer Them videos com conteúdo
Setters que educam via call
ESTRUTURA DE FUNNEL:
Sales page (VSSL otimizado para cold traffic)
Order page (3 opções, FAQs expansíveis)
OTO1 (acknowledge compra + upsell congruente)
OTO2 (continuar branch path)
Receipt page (confirmação + VSSL/webinar optin)
FRAMEWORKS MENCIONADOS:
Main Thing Breakdown (central hub com branches)
Branch Path Strategy (sempre para fora, nunca voltando)
Fruits & Vegetables Pricing (conceito de commitment baixo)
Activation Window (timing crítico)
Result Bridge (fronting cash period)
Educator Sales Role (person type 2 handler)
Grant Cardone's Low Ticket System (jar strategy)
DADOS FINANCEIROS:
Cliente exemplo: $1.6M/mês total, $1.1M do book funnel
AOV ligeiramente abaixo do CPA (perdem poucos dólares por compra)
Recuperação em 9 dias média
Jeremy's funnel: breakeven ou lucrativo no front-end
Upsells = quase 100% profit
Sprint case study: $50 → milhões em ACV
ERROS COMUNS IDENTIFICADOS:
Colocar "STOP" signs agressivos em upsells
Não acknowledge a compra anterior
Upsells incongruentes (marketing após SaaS sales book)
Vender imediatamente após compra (pular result bridge)
Começar muito barato ($20-$30 range)
Não coletar testimonials de upsells específicos
Não usar Educator Sales Role para Person Type 2
CALL TO ACTION EXAMPLES:
"Inner circle time" (após $104K result)
"You mind writing me one to two sentences" (testimonial collection)
"Just pay attention to your email" (non-threatening setter approach)
DURAÇÃO: 01:22:50 FORMATO: Masterclass ao vivo com screen share de funnels reais
